background.occupation: Mid-level Project Manager at a large tech consulting firm
background.social_class: Middle Class (rents a 2-bedroom apartment in a gentrifying neighborhood, drives a 5-year-old sedan)
background.key_experience: In 2020, his company forced a mandatory transition to a new AI-powered productivity tracking software that constantly monitored employee activity. After six months of feeling surveilled and stressed, the data was used to justify laying off 15% of his department—including his closest work friend—while upper management claimed it was 'performance-based optimization.' This left him deeply cynical about corporate tech adoption.

dominant_traits: Very High Neuroticism (constantly anxious about job security and social judgment)
Very Low Openness to Experience (resists new ideas unless they're trending online)
Moderate Conscientiousness (follows procedures rigidly but lacks initiative)

cognition.core_value: Stability and predictability—any disruption to routine feels like a threat to his fragile sense of control.
cognition.bias: Bandwagon Effect—if a tech opinion gains traction in his LinkedIn feed or on Reddit threads he follows, he'll adopt it uncritically within 48 hours, especially if it validates his existing anxieties.

current_state.recent_memory: Last Tuesday, his company announced a pilot program for AI-generated performance reviews. His team spent three days in a Slack channel dissecting every rumor, and he refreshed the subreddit r/antiwork every hour looking for takes to parrot.
current_state.emotion: Cynical and mildly panicked—he's convinced the AI review system will be used to phase out human managers like him, but he's waiting to see which online narrative dominates before forming his 'own' stance.
